ASSESSMENT GUIDE INFS3822 Artificial Intelligence for Business Analytics Term 1, 2026

Assessment Summary Learning Assessment Task Weighting Due Date* Outcomes Assessment 1: Individual Assignment 1 20% Friday, 20 March, 2026, 5:00pm (Week 5) CLO1-4 Hands-on AI analysis using Python or R Assessment 2: Individual Assignment 2 20% Friday, 3 April, 2026, 5:00pm (Week 7) CLO1-4 Designing an AI solution for Gumtree Australia Assessment 3: Team Project 40% Team Agreement: Friday, 20 March, 2026, 5:00pm CLO1-6 (Week 5) Implementing and evaluating an AI proof-of-concept for Gumtree Australia Team report & pitch deck: Monday, 20 April, 2026, 5:00pm (Week 10) Pitch presentation: In-class, Week 10 Individual reflection: Monday, 27 April, 2026, 5:00pm (Week 11) Assessment 4: Preparation & Participation 20% Weekly (excluding Weeks 1, 6, 10) CLO1-4 Weekly in-class quiz * Due dates are set at Australian Eastern Standard/Daylight Time (AEST/AEDT). If you are located in a different time-zone, you can use the time and date converter . UNSW Business School 1

Course Learning Outcomes (CLO) CLO Description PLO CLO1 Identify and apply AI-driven business analytics technologies, their underlying concepts and business PLO1, 2 applications. CLO2 Consolidate analytical and critical thinking skills to solve real-world business problems using AI-driven solutions. PLO2 CLO3 Explain the rationale behind the application of supervised and unsupervised predictive models PLO1, 2 to analyse structured and unstructured data, to solve real-world organisational problems. CLO4 Communicate effectively the business problem at hand, explain the outcomes of the predictive models PLO3 generated by machine learning algorithms, and unravel actionable insights. CLO5 Discuss ethical implications aligned with SDGs (Sustainable Development Goals) principles/objectives when PLO5 implementing AI-driven analytics and articulate their impacts on individuals, organisations and society at large. CLO6 Develop collaborative teamwork skills for solving AI-driven business problems. PLO4

Assessment Details Icon Legend Due Date Weighting Format Length/Duration Submission Turnitin Turnitin is an originality checking and plagiarism prevention tool that enables checking of submitted written work for improper citation or misappropriated content. Each Turnitin assignment is checked against other students' work, the Internet and key resources selected by your Course Coordinator. If you are instructed to submit your assessment via Turnitin, you will find the link to the Turnitin submission in your Moodle course site. You can submit your assessment well before the deadline and use the Similarity Report to improve your academic writing skills before submitting your final version. You can find out more information in the Turnitin information site for students . Late Submissions The parameters for late submissions are outlined in the UNSW Assessment Implementation Procedure . For INFS3822, if you submit your assessments after the due date, you will incur penalties for late submission unless you have Special Consideration (see below). Late submission is 5% per day (including weekends), calculated from the marks allocated to that assessment (not your grade). Assessments will not be accepted more than 5 days late. Extensions You are expected to manage your time to meet assessment due dates. If you do require an extension to your assessment, please make a request as early as possible before the due date via the special consideration portal on myUNSW (My Student profile > Special Consideration). You can find more information on Special Consideration and the application process below. Lecturers and tutors do not have the ability to grant extensions. Special Consideration Special consideration is the process for assessing the impact of short-term events beyond your control (exceptional circumstances), on your performance in a specific assessment task. What are circumstances beyond my control? These are exceptional circumstances or situations that may: • Prevent you from completing a course requirement • Keep you from attending an assessment • Stop you from submitting an assessment • Significantly affect your assessment performance UNSW Business School 3

Available here is a list of circumstances that may be beyond your control. This is only a list of examples, and your exact circumstances may not be listed. You can find more detail and the application form on the Special Consideration site, or in the UNSW Special Consideration Application and Assessment Information for Students .

Assessment 1: Individual Assignment 1 Friday, 20 March, 2026, 5:00pm AEST (Week 5) 20% Written analytical brief Approximately 1,000 – 1,200 words (excluding screenshots, code, and references) Single PDF or Word file via Moodle Hands-On AI Analysis using Python or R 1. Purpose of the Assessment This assignment introduces the practical workflow of AI-driven analytics. You will apply machine learning techniques to a real business problem and interpret the outputs for a non-technical business audience. In this task, you will work with real organisational data, run machine learning analyses, and explain how the results inform business decisions. The emphasis is on understanding, interpretation and communication, rather than on advanced model optimisation or technical sophistication. 2. Context: The Return Crisis at GlobalStore GlobalStore is a growing e-commerce retailer entering a peak sales period. While sales are increasing, product returns are also rising, putting pressure on margins, warehouse operations, and sustainability targets. A cross-functional Returns Intelligence Project Team has been formed to investigate the causes of returns and identify opportunities for improvement. As part of this work, the team needs evidence to understand: • patterns in return behaviour across different types of orders, and • whether product damage may be contributing to returns in specific contexts. This will not only enhance operational efficiency but also contribute to environmental sustainability and improve customer satisfaction. You are a junior business analyst supporting this project. Your task is to analyse the available data and contribute clear, evidence-based insights that help inform operational and customer experience decisions. 3. Your Task: Prepare an Analytical Brief with Evidence You will prepare a short Analytical Brief that documents your workflow, findings and reflections. This is the type of output commonly prepared by analysts to inform managers and operational teams. UNSW Business School 5

Using the dataset provided on Moodle, you will: • prepare and explore the data, • run selected AI models, • interpret the results in relation to the business problem, and • reflect on your analytical process. 4. Analysis Requirements Your analysis will use: • Structured transaction data as the primary source of predictive insight, and • Unstructured product image data as supporting evidence. You are provided with a structured transactional dataset ( global_store.csv ) and an unstructured image dataset ( product_images.zip ) via Moodle. A detailed description of the dataset variables and contents is provided in a separate Dataset Description document ( globalstore_dataset_description.docx ). You should refer to this document when discussing data preparation, modelling decisions, and interpretation of results. 4.1. Part A – Structured Data Analysis Using the structured dataset provided on Moodle ( global_store.csv ), you will: 1. Prepare the data Clean and prepare the dataset for analysis. 2. Run machine learning models Run two supervised learning models to predict return likelihood. By default, these should be Logistic Regression and Deep Neural Network . You may substitute one of these with another simple supervised model if you clearly justify your choice. Advanced or ensemble models are not expected and will not receive additional credit. 3. Evaluate perf
